---
title: "Preface"
number-sections: false
---

# About This Book {.unnumbered}

**BLISS-2026** is an updated thesis that takes the *Bi-Level Integrated System
Synthesis* (BLISS) framework from its 2002 origins into the modern era of
surrogate-based optimization, Gaussian processes, and high-performance Julia computing.

The starting point is the author's 2002 Master's thesis completed at George Washington
University under Dr. Jaroslaw Sobieszczanski-Sobieski at NASA Langley Research Center,
published as NASA Contractor Report NASA/CR-2002-211652 [@altus2002blissrs]. That work
introduced *BLISS-RS*: quadratic response surface surrogates as the coupling mechanism
between local disciplinary optimizations and a global system optimizer.

Twenty-four years later, the surrogate-modeling landscape has changed fundamentally.
This book asks: if you were designing BLISS today, what would you do differently?

## What Changed Since 2002

The 2002 implementation used quadratic polynomials as surrogates because they were
cheap to fit, analytically differentiable, and computationally trivial. Those
properties still matter — but the toolkit has expanded significantly:

| Then (2002) | Now (2026) |
|-------------|-----------|
| Quadratic polynomial surrogates | Gaussian Process (Kriging) surrogates |
| Least-squares fit, fixed once | Marginal-likelihood hyperparameter tuning |
| D-Optimal DOE, fixed budget | Latin Hypercube + adaptive Expected Improvement |
| Nelder-Mead over polynomial | Bayesian optimization with EI acquisition |
| No uncertainty estimates | Full predictive distributions |
| Serial BB evaluation | Parallel `Threads.@threads` in Julia |
| Fixed 20% interval shrinkage | Trust-region bounds management |

## What This Book Contains

The first eight chapters closely follow the original 2002 thesis — faithfully
reproduced with all algorithms implemented in Julia. They establish the classical
BLISS-RS framework in complete, executable detail.

The modern contribution begins at Chapter 9:

- **Chapter 9 — BLISS-2026 Framework**: The full Gaussian-process-based redesign.
  GP theory, kernel functions, marginal likelihood, Expected Improvement acquisition,
  and a complete working implementation that runs the supersonic business jet
  benchmark. Direct comparison against BLISS-RS shows where GP surrogates win and
  where they add cost.

- **Chapter 10 — Modern MDO Landscape**: A survey of where MDO decomposition is
  heading: multi-fidelity co-kriging, neural-network surrogates, uncertainty
  quantification, and digital-twin integration.

## Running This Book

```bash
# Requires Julia 1.10+ (brew install julia) and pixi (pixi.sh)
pixi run setup    # one-time: install Julia packages
pixi run render   # build HTML → docs/
pixi run preview  # live preview at localhost:4000
```

## Notation

| Symbol | Meaning |
|--------|---------|
| $Z$ | System-level design variables |
| $X$ | Local design variables (private to one BB) |
| $Y^*, Y^A$ | Coupling variable targets / actual outputs |
| $w$ | Weighting factors for synthetic local objective |
| $\mathbf{k}$ | GP kernel (covariance) function |
| $\mu(\cdot), \sigma^2(\cdot)$ | GP posterior mean and variance |
| $\text{EI}(\cdot)$ | Expected Improvement acquisition function |
| $\ell$ | GP length-scale hyperparameter |
| BB | Black box (disciplinary analysis module) |
| RS | Response surface (quadratic polynomial surrogate) |
| GP | Gaussian process surrogate |
| DOE | Design of experiments |
| LHS | Latin Hypercube Sampling |

## Citation

If you use material from this work, please cite both the original report and this book:

> Altus, T. D., "A Response Surface Methodology for Bi-Level Integrated System
> Synthesis (BLISS)," NASA/CR-2002-211652, NASA Langley Research Center, May 2002.

> Altus, T. D., *BLISS-2026: Decomposition-Based MDO — A Modern Julia Treatment*,
> 2026. [altustd.github.io/bliss2026](https://altustd.github.io/bliss2026)

---

*Troy David Altus · 2026*
